does: This paper introduces goal alignment as a reformulation of the value alignment problem, grounded in the observation that misalignment often stems not from reward function specification mechanics but from asymmetries between human expectations of agent behavior and actual agent behavior. The authors identify a foundational cause of misalignment: users have implicit expectations about how agents should behave that diverge from what their explicitly specified objectives produce. By proposing goal alignment, the work shifts focus from technical issues in reward specification toward reconciling these human-agent behavioral expectations. The paper bridges value alignment research with human-aware planning, proposing a framework that accounts for how human mental models of agent goals diverge from formal objective specifications.
connects: This work extends the value alignment literature, which has historically emphasized difficulties in translating human preferences into computable reward functions. Prior research by Hadfield-Menell, Russell, and others addressed specification gaming and reward hacking, but treated alignment primarily as a technical representation problem. Goal alignment builds on concurrent human-aware AI planning research that emphasizes agent transparency and interpretability. The paper also draws on human-robot interaction and human-AI collaboration work showing that humans have strong intuitive expectations about agent rationality and behavior. By synthesizing these areas, the work responds to limitations in purely formalist approaches to alignment that ignore the pragmatic gap between formal objectives and human expectations about what those objectives should entail in practice.
enables: This reframing opens pathways for developing alignment approaches that incorporate human mental models and behavioral expectations as first-class components rather than afterthoughts. It suggests future work on learning implicit human goals through behavioral observation and expectation modeling, developing agents that make their objective-goal mappings transparent to users, and creating feedback mechanisms that surface misalignments in expectations before deployment. The goal alignment framework could enable more human-centered design of AI systems where agents actively manage human expectations and clarify their interpretations of objectives. This approach also points toward formal tools for reasoning about the relationship between stated goals and expected behavior, potentially informing safety verification methods that account for human-agent interpretation gaps.
